title: The Science of Dosage: Why Milligrams Per Kilogram Matters
body:
The Science of Dosage: Why Milligrams Per Kilogram Matters

When you hear someone talking about dosing psychedelics, microdosing, or any medication really, you might catch phrases like “take 10 milligrams” or “start with 5 mg.” But here’s the thing: that number alone doesn’t tell you the whole story. Why? Because your body size, your weight, actually changes how much of that substance you need to reach the intended effect. This is where the concept of milligrams per kilogram (mg/kg) comes into play.

Let’s get real for a moment. You and your friend might both have 5 mg of something in front of you, but if your friend weighs 60 kg and you weigh 90 kg, that same 5 mg dose hits you two very differently. Dose doesn’t exist in a vacuum. It’s not just about the number. It’s about your body, your mass, your metabolism. Know what I mean?

What Does Milligrams Per Kilogram Actually Mean?

Simply put, milligrams per kilogram is a way to measure dose that accounts for your body weight. It divides the amount of a substance (in milligrams) by your weight (in kilograms). So, if you take 10 mg of a drug and you weigh 70 kg, your dose is about 0.14 mg/kg.

Why does that matter? Because many drugs, including psychedelics, act differently depending on how much of your body they have to work through. Your blood volume, fat content, and even liver enzyme activity can influence how a drug is absorbed and processed. The mg/kg system helps to create a more individualized, accurate dosing guideline.

What Happens When You Ignore This?

Picture this: two people, Alice and Bob, decide to microdose psilocybin. Alice weighs 55 kg, Bob weighs 95 kg. Both take 10 mg of a psilocybin extract. For Alice, that’s about 0.18 mg/kg. For Bob, it’s just over 0.10 mg/kg. The difference in their subjective experience could be huge. Alice might feel a light shift in perception and mood boost, while Bob might barely notice anything.

Ignoring mg/kg is like trying to give everyone the same size shoes. It just doesn’t fit. A dose that’s perfectly fine for someone lighter might be too intense for another, or vice versa. This is why you can’t just copy someone else’s dose and expect the same results.

How Pharmacology Uses mg/kg

In medical research and pharmacology, mg/kg is standard. Clinical trials often report doses in mg/kg because it helps researchers understand how a drug works across different body sizes. For example, ketamine infusions for depression often use a dose of 0.5 mg/kg. This means if you weigh 80 kg, you get 40 mg; if you weigh 60 kg, you get 30 mg. This method helps ensure everyone receives an effective and safe dose relative to their body.

Truth is, even in psychedelic therapy, this approach is gaining traction. Microdosing studies, like those on psilocybin or LSD, have started reporting dosage in mg/kg rather than absolute milligrams. It’s about making dosing safer, more predictable, and tailored.

What About Microdosing?

Microdosing is often described as taking about 1/10th to 1/20th of a typical recreational dose. But without considering body weight, that fraction is just a guess. Let’s say a standard active dose of psilocybin is 20 mg. A microdose then might be around 1 mg to 2 mg. But for someone who weighs 50 kg versus 90 kg, that microdose could mean very different things.

By using mg/kg, you can figure out the microdose relative to your body. For example, 0.03 mg/kg is a number that some researchers have suggested as a starting microdose for psilocybin. That means a 50 kg person would take 1.5 mg, while a 90 kg person would take 2.7 mg. Small difference? Maybe. Big difference? Possibly, especially over time.

Here’s the Thing: You’re Not Just Weight

Okay, so mg/kg is a better guide than just milligrams. But it’s not the full picture. Metabolism, genetics, diet, and even your gut microbiome can influence how a drug behaves in your body. That’s why dosing is part art, part science. You want to start low and go slow, monitor your responses, and adjust accordingly.

Still, mg/kg gives you a solid baseline. It’s a more scientific way to approach dosing that respects your unique biology. It’s not guesswork. It’s a formula.

How to Calculate Your Dose Using mg/kg

Here’s a simple way to figure it out:

  • Step 1: Know your weight in kilograms. If you weigh in pounds, divide by 2.2 to convert.
  • Step 2: Find the recommended mg/kg dose for the substance you’re interested in.
  • Step 3: Multiply your weight (kg) by the mg/kg dose.
  • Step 4: That’s your target dose in milligrams.

For example, if you weigh 70 kg and want to try a ketamine infusion at 0.5 mg/kg, multiply 70 by 0.5 and you get 35 mg.

How This Changes Your Microdosing Practice

Let’s say you’ve been microdosing 10 mg of a psilocybin extract every three days. You weigh 80 kg. That’s 0.125 mg/kg. If your goal is to microdose at 0.03 mg/kg, you’re actually taking more than four times the recommended microdose. That could explain why you’re feeling more effects than “just a microdose.”

Adjusting dosage this way can help you tune your experience, reduce unwanted side effects, and increase benefits. You might also find that your mental clarity improves, that you sleep better, or that your mood lifts gently but steadily.

If you want a tool to track these doses, a therapy journal with guided prompts (paid link) can be a shift. Writing down your doses, effects, and feelings helps you notice patterns and make smarter adjustments over time.

Does That Mean Everyone Should Dose by mg/kg?

Honestly, yes and no. Weight-based dosing is a strong starting point, but it’s not the only factor. Your sensitivity to chemicals, tolerance, and the context of your experience can all shift how a dose hits you. For example, someone with a history of anxiety might need a lower dose than their mg/kg calculation suggests.

Plus, different psychedelics have different pharmacokinetics. LSD works at microgram levels, while psilocybin is dosed in milligrams. So you’ll need to know your substance and the research behind it.

Look, there’s no perfect formula for human experience. But starting with mg/kg makes dosing smarter. Safer. More you.

Real-World Example: Psilocybin Therapy

Take recent clinical trials on psilocybin for depression. These studies often calculate doses based on weight. In one study published in the National Institutes of Health database, patients received 25 mg or 0.3 mg/kg of psilocybin. The dosing was adjusted so heavier patients didn’t get underdosed and lighter patients weren’t overdosed. The results? Better symptom relief across the board, fewer side effects, and more consistent outcomes.

That’s the power of mg/kg dosing. It makes sure you get what you need, no more no less. Does that land?

Practical Tips for Your Next Psychedelic Session

  • Know your weight in kilograms before dosing.
  • Find reliable mg/kg dosing info from trusted sources or clinical research.
  • Start at the low end of the range, especially if it’s your first time.
  • Track your doses and effects in a journal. Need one? Grab a therapy journal with guided prompts (paid link).
  • Prepare your space with tools that help you relax and integrate, like a gentle meditation timer (paid link) or a silk sleep eye mask (paid link).
  • Stay hydrated and keep a trusted sitter or guide nearby.
  • Remember, you’re not alone. Reach out if you need support.

Why This Matters Beyond Psychedelics

This mg/kg dosing concept isn’t just for psychedelics. It’s how most medications are dosed in hospitals. Antibiotics, chemotherapy, anesthesia - all are calculated based on body weight. If you’ve ever had a surgery or taken strong meds, chances are your doctor considered mg/kg to protect you.

So when you’re exploring conscious healing, microdosing, or psychedelic wellness, bringing this kind of rigorous thinking can save you from pitfalls. It’s a small detail with a big impact.

Want to Learn More?

Check out my in-depth piece on microdosing best practices for more guidance on how to approach your dosing safely and effectively. Also, if you’re interested in stories from people who’ve navigated dosing themselves, my site has plenty of honest, practical advice and reflections.

For a compelling read on the personal effects of microdosing and the science behind it, you might want to pick up A Really Good Day by Ayelet Waldman (paid link). It’s a vivid reminder that dosage and timing really change everything.

Final Thoughts

Look, dosing isn’t a one-size-fits-all number. It’s a calculation, a starting point, a conversation between your body and the medicine. The science of mg/kg is your friend here. It respects your individuality. It helps you avoid guesswork.

So next time you plan your dose, ask yourself: how many milligrams per kilogram am I actually taking? That question could change your entire experience. And honestly, that’s worth the few minutes it takes to calculate.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
